  text-align: center; /* Center the pagination */
  margin: 20px auto; /* Center the element and add vertical spacing */
}

.page-links a {
  color: #0066cc; /* Blue color for links */
  background-color: #f0f0f0; /* Light grey background for links */
  text-decoration: none; /* Remove underline from links */
  padding: 10px 20px; /* Add padding to make links larger and clickable */
  border-radius: 10px; /* Rounded corners */
  margin: 0 5px; /* Space between links */
  transition: background-color 0.3s ease, color 0.3s ease; /* Smooth transition for hover effects */
  display: inline-block; /* Ensure proper padding and margin */
  font-size: 18px; /* Larger font size for readability */
}

.page-links a:hover {
  background-color: #cce7ff; /* Light blue background on hover */
  color: #004080; /* Darker blue text on hover */
}

.page-links a.active {
  background-color: #004080; /* Dark blue background for active link */
  color: #ffffff; /* White text for active link */
  font-weight: bold; /* Bold text for active link */
}

.page-links::before {
  
  color: #333333; /* Grey color for the prefix text */
  font-size: 18px; /* Same font size as the links */
  margin-right: 10px; /* Space between prefix text and links */
}</style></head><body class="wp-singular post-template-default single single-post postid-3929 single-format-standard wp-embed-responsive wp-theme-generatepress post-image-below-header itemprop="headline">Paper and Pulp Notes | Class 12 Applied Chemistry Unit 20</h1></header><div class="entry-content" itemprop="text"><table><tbody><tr><th><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">Unit 20</span></th><th><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">Paper and Pulp</span></th></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">20.1</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">Introduction</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">20.2</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">Raw materials</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">20.3</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">Sources of raw materials</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">20.4</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">Stages in production of paper</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">20.5</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">Flow-sheet diagram for paper production</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">20.6</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;">Quality of paper</span></td></tr></tbody></table><h2 style="text-align: left;"><div style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Applied Chemistry&nbsp;</span></div><p><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"></p><div style="text-align: center;">Unit 20: Paper and Pulp</div><p></span></h2><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">In today’s world, paper is essential material for human being. It is used for book, writing, newsprint, packaging, tissue etc. <a href="https://www.caseyprinting.com/blog/how-is-paper-made" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Formation of paper </a>involves mainly two steps:</span></p><p><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><ol><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Pulping</b></span></li><li><b>Paper making</b></li></ol></ul><div><div style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgdL_aBHQhXVq3hrb7i6gZp2JNbLmU16mHfeSS4v8ObhpNfU8C715LuI7fUCE327cKoYdgO9DNE546jFj39On_p0YNPstlO14hgTXIQ0oNMgtzNAgW_csXLkJIjPDuO20UnBx28Zq_2VNeb516EVXHuzeGqRVXFpKkDbQCLtuA1N0VLRfA1gSOvfdX6//Paper-and-Pulp.png" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><img decoding="async" alt="Paper and Pulp Notes | Class 12 Applied Chemistry" border="0" data-original-height="720" data-original-width="1280" src="https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgdL_aBHQhXVq3hrb7i6gZp2JNbLmU16mHfeSS4v8ObhpNfU8C715LuI7fUCE327cKoYdgO9DNE546jFj39On_p0YNPstlO14hgTXIQ0oNMgtzNAgW_csXLkJIjPDuO20UnBx28Zq_2VNeb516EVXHuzeGqRVXFpKkDbQCLtuA1N0VLRfA1gSOvfdX6/s16000-rw/Paper-and-Pulp.png" title="Paper and Pulp Notes" /></a></div></div><p></span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Pulp:</b> <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pulp_(paper)" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Pulp</a> is a lignocellulosic fibrous material prepared chemically or mechanically by separating cellulose fibres from wood, fibre crops, waste paper or rags.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Paper:</b> Paper is the aqueous deposit of any cellulose fibre (wood, fibre crops) in sheet form.&nbsp;Generally, bamboo, straw, jute, waste paper etc. are used to make paper.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Historically, the first paper-like plant-based writing sheet was called Papyrus found in Egypt (4th century) but the first true paper was documented in China during Eastern Han Period.</span></p><h2 style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">RAW MATERIALS FOR PAPER INDUSTRY</span></h2></div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Raw materials for paper making include synthetic, mineral or plant fibres. Most of the paper is manufactured using pulp from wood.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">The raw materials which are used to prepare paper is pulp. The materials used to prepare pulp are:</span></p><div><ol style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Fibrous raw materials</b></span></li><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Non-Fibrous raw materials</b></span></li></ol><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">1. Fibrous raw materials</span></h3></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Conventional material type:</b> bamboo, Wood, etc.</span></li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Soft wood:</b>&nbsp;Coniferous and non-coniferous wood e.g. pines.</span></li></ul></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Hard woods:</b>&nbsp;Eucalyptus, mulberry, rubber plant wood etc.</span></li></ul></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Non-Conventional material type:</b> rice straw, wheat straw, etc.</span></li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Waste product type:</b> <a href="https://bioresourcesbioprocessing.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40643-021-00440-z" target="_blank" rel="noopener">sugar bagasse</a>, <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sawdust" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Sawdust</a>, etc</span></li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Cotton type:</b> cotton, cotton rags, jute, etc.</span></li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Grass and reeds:</b>&nbsp;a tall plant with hollow stem e.g. Lemon, bamboo etc.</span></li></ul></ul></div><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">2. Non-Fibrous raw materials</span></h3><div style="text-align: left;"><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Inorganic raw materials:</b> Na2S, NaOH, TiO2, etc.</span></li></ul><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Organic raw materials:</b> Wax, glycerols, glue, etc.</span></li></ul></ul></div><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Sources of Raw materials</span></h3><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">A major source of pulp is woods. The sources of Raw materials used in paper production classified as:</span></p><div><p style="text-align: left;"><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Wood Type:</b> Hard wood like gum, paper mulberry, rubber plants etc &amp; Soft wood like pine, fir, etc.</span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Grass Type:</b> Lemon grass, reeds, siru, babiyo, kush, etc</span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Straw Type:</b> It is based on rice, wheat, barley, etc.</span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Waste product:</b> This includes bagasse, sawdust, etc.</span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><b>Recycling materials:</b> Various types of waste paper can be recycled for the preparation of paper.</span></li></ul><div><p style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: medium;"><i><b><span>Read more:&nbsp;</span></b><a href="https://www.narayannepal.com.np/2022/12/cement-notes-class-12-chemistry.html" style="text-align: left;" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cement<span>&nbsp;Notes | Class 12 Chemistry</span></a></i></span></p></div></div><h2 style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">STAGES IN PRODUCTION OF PAPER</span></h2><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">The main stages in production of paper are</span></p><p><span style="font-family: verdana;"><span style="font-size: large;"></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul></ul></p></ul><p></span><span style="font-size: large;"></p><p style="text-align: left;"><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-size: large;">Raw material preparation and handling</span></li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li>Preparation of fiber suspension ( pulp manufacturing)</li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li>Bleaching process</li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li>Paper making procedure</li></ul></ul><p></span><span style="font-size: large;"></p><ul style="text-align: left;"></ul><p></span><span style="font-size: large;"></p><ul style="text-align: left;"></ul><p></span><span style="font-size: large;"></p><ul style="text-align: left;"></ul><p></span><span style="font-size: large;"></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul></ul><ul></ul><ul></ul></p></ul><p></span></span></div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">The general paper production can be explained details in the following steps:</span></p><div><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">1. Timbering</span></h3><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Timbers are obtained from mature trees. The <a href="https://www.quora.com/How-is-the-quality-of-paper-measured" target="_blank" rel="noopener">quality of paper</a> depends upon the quality of timber.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">2. De-barking</span></h3><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">It is the process of removing the bark of trees. This process is performed hydraulically with a high-pressure jet or mechanically by rubbing logs. Stripped timber is used for paper production.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">3. Chipping Process</span></h3><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Stripped timbers are chipped into small pieces using a rotatory disc and heavy knives. The chips are then digested in a digestor tower where maximum lignins are removed with minimum loss of cellulose.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">4. Pulping</span></h3></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">The disintegration of long and bulky fibrous materials into individual or small bulky mass is called pulping. There is mechanical, Chemical and <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7xH-lmsSU4o" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Hydropulping</a> method to produce pulp.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">It is the process by which the bond within the wood structure is ruptured either mechanically or chemically.</span></p><p><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"></p><p style="text-align: left;"><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Mechanical pulping process:</b> Here, rotatory steel discs with teeth are used to tear wood parts.</span></li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Chemical pulping process:</b> Here, wood chips are fed into a digestor and cookedto remove lignin. Here, chlorine, Sodium hypochlorite are used.</span></li></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Hydropulping:</b> Here, wood fibres are brought into a circular tank with a powerful agitator containing a water supply that breaks the wooden fibres into small pieces.</span></li></ul></ul><p></span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">5. Blend Chest</span></h3></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Chemicals are added to obtain desired features of the paper. In some cases dyes can be used to obtain the color of paper.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">6. Waste Paper Collect</span></h3></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Waste papers collected from commercial dumps are included here.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">7. De-inking</span></h3></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Here, the inks are removed from waste papers by washing.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">8. Refining</span></h3></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Cellulose fibres are stiff and inflexible. So, these cellulose fibres are refined to avoid these unwanted properties.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">9. Screening and Cleaning</span></h3></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Processed pulp may contain undesirable fibrous and non-fibrous material which should be cleaned. Centrifugal cleaners are employed here.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">10. Conversion and Printing</span></h3></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">The slurry of fibres is drained to prepare a continuous paper web which is passed through a pressing section to squeeze out excess water and dried.</span></p><h2 style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-size: large;"><span dir="ltr" role="presentation" style="font-family: verdana; left: 120px; top: 817.288px; transform: scaleX(1.07174);">FLOWSHEET DIAGRAM FOR PAPER PRODUCTION</span></span></h2><p><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span>The flowsheet for paper production is given as:</span></p><p></span></p><div style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhacbq8NGoROhluotEgtjq7vPViNw3OWhOtzn2Jrrsi-Lny1ttUuaHXqPQoFiPkvxQUIneMy2Rhz8gOfHwAuMEOU6Og0Mbb1G5a5ME4bpmTwxwz1t87cSwH33x3QRiWqlbJrZKicTbG-xvL8IV83dw30YCH1P_LQrWHHelA3SBlaJT9uuB0TsIGilGe/s2048-rw/FLOWSHEET-DIAGRAM-FOR-PAPER-PRODUCTION.png" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="font-family: verdana;"><img decoding="async" alt="FLOWSHEET DIAGRAM FOR PAPER PRODUCTION" border="0" data-original-height="1536" data-original-width="2048" src="https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhacbq8NGoROhluotEgtjq7vPViNw3OWhOtzn2Jrrsi-Lny1ttUuaHXqPQoFiPkvxQUIneMy2Rhz8gOfHwAuMEOU6Og0Mbb1G5a5ME4bpmTwxwz1t87cSwH33x3QRiWqlbJrZKicTbG-xvL8IV83dw30YCH1P_LQrWHHelA3SBlaJT9uuB0TsIGilGe/s16000-rw/FLOWSHEET-DIAGRAM-FOR-PAPER-PRODUCTION.png" title="FLOWSHEET DIAGRAM FOR PAPER PRODUCTION" /></span></a></div><p><span style="font-family: verdana;"><br /></span></p><h2 style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">QUALITY OF PAPER</span></h2><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">The quality of paper is determined based on the following characteristics:</span></p><p><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"></p><p style="text-align: left;"><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Brightness:</b> It means the percentage of light that paper reflects. Generally, coated papers have a better appearance than uncoated paper.</span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Colour</b></span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Finish:</b> It includes the characteristics of the surface of the paper.</span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Sizing:</b> It must be uniform so that when ink is applied, the lines appear clean.</span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Opacity:</b> It is related to the thickness of the paper.</span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Clarity</b></span></li></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-size: large;"><b>Weight:</b> The weight of paper is explained based on Grammage and is defined as the weight per square metre and expressed in gsm. Grammage up to 200 gsm are considered as paper whereas above 200 gsm are considered as paper board or low-quality board.</span></li></ul><p></span></p><h2 style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS OF PULP &amp; PAPER INDUSTRIES</span></h2></div><div><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Paper manufacturing has used up about 40% of all global wood, 4% of the world’s energy. The wastes released from paper and pulp industries like oxides of nitrogen, sulphur, carbon, chlorine-based bleaching agent contribute a lot to environmental pollution.</span></p><h2 style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">PULP AND PAPER INDUSTRIES IN NEPAL</span></h2><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana;"><span style="font-size: large;">4 decades of production of paper &amp; pulp has been recorded in context to Nepal. According to recent data; <b>46 paper &amp; pulp industries</b> are in operation at present. <b><a href="https://www.everestgroup.com.np/epm.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Everest Paper Mills Pvt.<br /></a></b></span><span style="font-size: large;"><b><a href="https://www.everestgroup.com.np/epm.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Ltd.</a> (1982, Janakpur)</b> is the <b>first paper manufacturing industry</b> in Nepal. Bhrikuti Pulp and Paper Nepal Limited (1985) was the 1st state-owned paper industry later was permanently closed in 2011.</span></span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">LOKTA PAPER</span></h3><p><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="font-size: large;">Nepali handmade paper made up of fibrous inner bark of high elevation evergreen shrubs namely Daphne bholua and Daphne papyracea.</span></li><li><span style="font-size: large;">Durability and resistance to tearing, humidity, insects have made lokta paper to be used widely in the Nepali legal sector as <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lokta_paper" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Nepali Kagaj</a> (नेपाली कागज).</span></li></ul><p></span></p><h2 style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Differences Between</span></h2><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">The difference between Sulphite Pulping process and <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uJcg-DIt_e0" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Kraft Pulping</a> process (Sulphate process) is given as:</span></p></div><table><tbody><tr><th><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Sulphite Pulping Process</span></th><th><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Kraft Pulping Process OR Sulphate Process</span></th></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">It is a technique used to produce wood pulp using sulphites or bisulphite salt of sodium, calcium, potassium, magnesium and ammonium.</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">It is a technique used to convert wood into wood pulp using a mixture of water, sodium hydroxide, sodium sulphide.</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">It produces stronger cellulose fibres.</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">It produces weak cellulose fibres.</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Its efficiency is high.</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Its efficiency is low.</span></td></tr><tr><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">It causes less harm to the environment.</span></td><td><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">It causes more harm to the environment.</span></td></tr></tbody></table><p style="text-align: center;"><b><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Read more:</span></b></p><p style="text-align: left;"><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><ul><li><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;"><a href="https://www.narayannepal.com.np/2022/12/cement-notes-class-12-chemistry.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cement<span>&nbsp;Notes | Class 12 Chemistry</span></a></span></li></ul></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><ul><li><a href="https://www.narayannepal.com.np/2022/08/haloarenes-class-12-chemistry.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Haloarenes Notes | Class 12 Chemistry</span></a></li></ul></ul></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"><ul><ul><li><a href="https://www.narayannepal.com.np/2022/08/volumetric-analysis-class-12.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="font-family: verdana; font-size: large;">Volumetric Analysis | Class 12 Chemistry</span></a></li></ul></ul></ul><p><span style="font-family: verdana;"><a href="https://www.narayannepal.com.np/2022/08/haloalkane-class-12-chemistry.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a></span></p><div><span style="font-family: verdana;"><br /></span></p><div><ul style="text-align: left;"></ul><ul style="text-align: left;"></ul></div></div><div style="text-align: center;"><span style="font-family: verdana;"><b><b><span style="font-size: medium;">Paper Production Animation by</span></b></b> <a href="https://www.youtube.com/@karthiexplains" target="_blank" rel="noopener">@karthiexplains</a></span></div><p> <span style="font-family: verdana;"><br /> <iframe allow="accelerometer;
